does: This paper introduces Selective Representation Misdirection for Unlearning (SRMU), an activation-editing framework designed to remove sensitive knowledge from large language models while preserving general utility. SRMU works by applying feature-aware and directionally controlled perturbations to model activations rather than weights. The method uses a structured misdirection vector combined with an activation importance map to selectively suppress target knowledge in specific neurons or feature channels. The approach addresses the practical challenge of unlearning when forget and retain datasets have entangled distributions, showing that targeted activation perturbations can achieve knowledge removal with minimal degradation to model performance on unrelated tasks.
connects: This work builds on recent machine unlearning literature that tackles the challenge of removing prohibited or sensitive information from deployed language models while maintaining their general capabilities. It responds to limitations in prior perturbation-based unlearning methods, which often cause substantial utility loss or fail to reliably prevent knowledge access when forget and retain datasets overlap significantly. SRMU extends the activation-editing approach by introducing principled feature selectivity, drawing on neuron-level interpretability research that identifies which model components encode specific concepts. The work addresses operational realities underappreciated in earlier unlearning studies, where perfect dataset separation is unrealistic in practice.
enables: This framework opens pathways for practical deployment of unlearning in regulated industries where models must comply with evolving data governance and safety requirements. The feature-selective activation approach could enable more precise control over which knowledge is forgotten without collateral damage to useful capabilities, supporting applications in privacy-critical domains like healthcare and finance. Future work could extend the method to multimodal models, develop automated identification of importance-weighted activation features for different knowledge types, and create efficient batch unlearning procedures. The principled activation-perturbation strategy may also inspire new defenses against adversarial knowledge extraction and inform the design of inherently unlearnable model architectures.
